author: @MisterkeTube
text: What I'm missing most in all these wind-turbine videos is some useful info on the power they would produce.  1 m^2 solar panel can give you 100W or so, but the turbines in these videos often take up way more room, but are often just shown lighting up a couple of LEDs, which honestly even a potato can do ...  Also people testing reasonably sized windmills are often disappointed with the actual power those produce.  So, although the mechanisms being explained make these videos great, but is the thing you're using these mechanisms then for actually worth it?

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Hey Robert.

That setup is used for local speed variation in chairlift stations, it's known as the "cadenceur" and is used to combat detachable grip chairs and cabins slpiing and changing relative places on the cable.  It's a fun mechanism.

For your uses, though, it still has slip, and if you want overlapping savonius turbines, the solution with the least loss would seem to me to be to gear them together and take a feed out somewhere.

(edit : the "its known as" refers to the part of the chairlift, not the mechanism itself, which is just a dual-driven planetary drive)

author: @VeniceInventors
text: Use magnets around horizontal discs at the base of the two savonius turbines, to both keep them in sync (acting like a magnetic clutch), and generate the power by having a coil cross the magnetic field of both discs, so you still have a single generator, and no friction losses.

author: @RaglansElectricBaboon
text: Watching this has led me to a completely different idea for dealing with the issue. Mechanical solutions always have losses and can breakdown. Go electrical. Get/create a control system which keeps the blades going at a constant speed by reducing the power draw during the part of the rotation where wind power is not being harvested.

author: @Pystro
text:
You actually DON'T want a differential in this situation. In order to overcome the low-torque phase of one wind turbine, it has to stay lined up with the high-torque phase of the other turbine (and vice versa). They _HAVE_ to be locked together, not linked via a differential.
Which is why stacking the turbines on top of each other is such an elegant solution: It's the easiest way to lock them together.

text: Why not use a timing belt between the two sails, which keeps the sails rotating without colliding. Then you can introduce a timing gear within the original belt or use an extended shaft on one of the sails and drive a separate gear to the generator, you coould even make the gears to increase the speed to the generator. I was a technical manager in a company which produced different sizes of hypercycloidal gearboxes often with multiple stages plus other forms of gears and gearboxes. Good post thank you 👍☺️

author: @JasonBurchell76
text:
For the vertically oriented, colorful vanes you showed rotating in opposite directions, you could use the planetary setup used in aircraft with ContraRotating Props but instead of have a static coupling , introduce a roller clutch around the cage of the planetary assembly. What’s more, for a vertical configuration, they would be very easily stackable to allow for more vanes to be attached. 
Then, as suggested by another one of your viewers, use a hydrostatic type drive to transmit the power to  the generators. You can also get a lot of torque multiplication using hydrostatic drives. 
There’s a view printable models of the contra rotating propellers on Thingiverse and Printables if you need to see it in person. 
Awesome video as always. Thanks

author: @joeschmo622
text:
If solar panels use multiple microinverters, can't multiple turbines do the same?
If there'd be a need to fix turbines together mechanically, why not a bicycle chain and gears?  Each turbine in a pair gets the same sized gear, "slip" the chain until they're synced the way you want them, and a power-take-off gear in the middle (can be any size to multiply speed or torque as wanted) goes to the generator.

author: @jamesross1003
text: First thing that comes to mind is a solution to wind wall problems.  One of these on each column with an intermediate gear between and you would have additive torque for each pair of columns at an average speed.  I can see real potential there as a solution to many issues with turbines and wind walls among many other things.  My grandson is currently in his school's program for advanced health education.  This does include use and creation of machines that improve surgery and treatment.  I think I'll pass this video on to him as an issue right now in robotic medicine is a robotic stitching machine which is difficult to get two or more feet of the machine in sync to walk/pull itself across a wound without a lot of processing.  I can see this as a jump off point for something like that.  Who knows, but any solution might beat what is currently a bad one.  With your blessings of course.  Thanks Robert!  I am kind of a clockwork nut, so this is something I really enjoy.  Appreciate the videos Robert, please continue.  Thanks again!

author: @VacuumTube88
text: The questionit comes to mind is then; would the increased mecchanical complexity worth it for  that type of combined turbine?  Could a different approach like with 2 separate turbines mecchanically locked with a chain or belt gear and each a generator connected in series with a simple standoff diode and a zener diode and resitor to act as a votage regulator be easier? I mean that gearing needs to be made from something sturdier and more durable than PLA so it adds cost and complexity, who choses 2 bladed diy Savonius turbines have as their objective, low cost, simplicity (bot construction and repair) and durability.

replies.author: @dbmail545
replies.text: @ksbrugh9886  friction losses in hydraulic systems is directly related to length of plumbing (how close the driven device has to be to the driver) and the plumbing cross section (larger cross section=less friction and greater volume of oil)

author: @grendel1960a
text: you could clock the two turbines using a chain link or drive band the only issue is that they would then be turning equally and giving no output, if you put a cross in the drive band between them that would enable them to contra rotate and remain clocked, or possibly use a toothed belt for more stability and less slip- the cross in the toothed belt could be seet so the back of the belt passed the back of the other section avoiding catching.

author: @cr0w-qz277
text:
The edge-on problem is probably the smallest that VAWT faces - the greater issue is that at a 180d turn, the wind is actively pushing back against the rotation of the turnbine, sapping energy from the system. 

Granted, sybonius turbines are still grwat for simplicity and i'd take 100 of them over 1-2 HAWT for generation purposes, but they arent exactly maximally efficient.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: tbh mate sometimes maximum efficiency isn't what matters
